concepts.title: Types of Drainage System
concepts.description: There are two main types of drainage systems: (1) Fluvial or Running Water Drainage and (2) Pluvial or Surface Water Drainage. Fluvial drainage involves the flow of water through rivers and streams, while pluvial drainage involves the flow of water from the land surface into streams and rivers.

concepts.title: Components of Drainage System
concepts.description: The components of a drainage system include the source (where water originates), channel (rivers, streams, etc.), confluence (where two or more streams meet), and mouth (where a river empties into another water body).

concepts.title: Process of Drainage
concepts.description: The process of drainage involves the collection and flow of water from the land surface into streams and rivers, and eventually into oceans. This process helps to shape the landscape and create various landforms.

concepts.title: Drainage Basin
concepts.description: A drainage basin is an area of land that drains water into a specific river or stream. It is also known as a catchment area.

concepts.title: River System
concepts.description: To remember the order of a river system, use the mnemonic: 'Source-Channel-Confluence-Mouth' (SCCM).

concepts.title: Difference between Drainage and Water Course
concepts.description: Students often confuse the terms 'drainage' and 'water course'. Drainage refers to the flow of water from the land surface into streams and rivers, while a water course is a natural or artificial channel through which water flows.

concepts.title: Drainage Units
concepts.description: Important drainage units to remember include: river, stream, brook, rivulet, and nullah. Each of these units has a specific role in the drainage system.

concepts.title: Role of Drainage System
concepts.description: The drainage system plays a crucial role in shaping the landscape, creating landforms, and influencing the climate and economy of an area.
